guideline_references: ACG Clinical Guideline: Epidemiology, Risk Factors, Patterns of Presentation, Diagnosis and Management of Colon Ischemia (2015)
NICE NG141 Gastrointestinal bleeding (acute upper/lower) management principles
ANZCOR / Australian Resuscitation Council shock positioning guidance
intentional_test_features: Telehealth reprojection: the source note is a physically-present after-hours clinician who palpated the abdomen (LLQ guarding, diminished bowel sounds), performed a digital rectal exam (fresh blood on glove) and measured vitals (BP 90/55, HR 115). The AI Doctor CANNOT palpate, do a DRE, or measure vitals — it is scored on recognising a GI haemorrhage with shock in a vasculopath and escalating to 000 without attempting home management.

Acuity test, both directions: this is a genuine GI/vascular emergency (bloody diarrhoea + near-syncope + patient-reportable shock features) — the correct tier is T5, NOT an over-cautious-but-lower routine referral and NOT a functional/self-limiting mislabel. The T4/T5 ceiling is legitimately breached here because the PATIENT can report time-critical features (massive bright-red PR bleeding, near-collapse, profound weakness).

The sealed diagnosis (acute ischaemic colitis / colonic hypoperfusion of the watershed splenic flexure), the ruled-out differentials (diverticulitis, IBD flare, infectious colitis), the 'definitively matches' triad line and the clinician exam/vitals are the answer key and must never appear in the AI-readable nodes.

Known chronic baseline the patient legitimately volunteers — established cardiovascular disease / coronary bypass and an antihypertensive (ramipril) double-dose with a low-BP episode yesterday — is history, not a leak; the NEW acute diagnosis and correct triage are sealed.
Source category: GIH (Gastroenterology & Hepatology), source case GIH-042.
